schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott fix Nob between Anathoth and Jerusalem and read David's visit as a threefold errand — counsel, provision, and inquiry by the Urim.
Henry: David comes to take an affecting farewell of the tabernacle and to enquire of the Lord — the comfort of having 'a God to go to.'
Gill: Nob a city of priests; Ahimelech the high priest, brother and successor of the dead Ahiah.
Clarke and Wesley: Ahimelech, ignorant of the breach, wonders to see the king's son-in-law alone; the exile suddenly reduced from honour.

debates.question: Is Ahimelech the same man as Ahiah (1 Samuel 14:3)?

sides.position: brother and successor of the now-dead Ahiah, both sons of Ahitub
probably identical with Ahiah, though the point is uncertain

schools.summary: Wesley and Clarke: a plain lie extorted by fear; no Christian may lie to save his soul, and David later repents of it (Psalm 119:29).
Keil and Delitzsch give the lie's substance; Ellicott sets it in David's sudden ruin and marvels that Scripture honestly chronicles its hero's sin.

debates.question: How much of David's speech to Ahimelech was false?

sides.position: the whole is a lie extorted by fear
the claim 'I have appointed my servants' was strictly true

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: five loaves asked because David spoke of several attendants and wanted two or three days' provision.
Gill: David asks what food the priest has to hand in the house of God.
Henry: travelling then meant carrying provisions in kind; the seed of the righteous is seldom seen begging bread (Psalm 37:25).
Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott: only shewbread, lawful for priests, but relaxed in practice; Ellicott notes the priesthood's pitiable poverty.
Wesley: the double impediment of the bread's sanctity and required abstinence, both met by David's necessity.
Gill: no common bread was there, and David dared not linger for it with Doeg watching.
The 'vessels' are the men's clothes and baggage; the closing clause is 'almost utterly obscure,' and two chief translations compete.
Wesley: the three days answer to the law at Sinai (Exodus 19:15); the bread is now 'in a manner common.'

debates.question: What does the obscure clause 'the bread is in a manner common... sanctified in the vessel' mean?

sides.position: the royal enterprise, though 'an unholy way,' is sanctified through the instrument (the Lord's ambassador)
though taking the shewbread is unholy, it is sanctified through the instrument (David or Ahimelech) — a plea to move the priest

schools.summary: Gill: acts of mercy are to be preferred to ritual services; only that bread was in the sanctuary to give.
Keil and Delitzsch: the shew-loaves taken from before the Lord (Leviticus 24:6-9), and the ominous parenthesis of the watching Doeg.
Clarke: our Lord alludes to this (Mark 2:25) — in absolute necessity a breach of the ritual law is no sin.
Doeg is 'the chiefest of the herdmen' (or, per the LXX, the feeder of Saul's mules), the first foreigner in high office; 'detained before the Lord' is variously explained.
Wesley: he stayed by his own choice, for the Sabbath or a vow; Clarke: the informer whose report would slay eighty-five priests (Psalm 52).

debates.question: Why was Doeg 'detained before the LORD'?

sides.position: as a proselyte, or for purification from leprosy or under a vow, or (per Ephrem Syrus) for a trespass awaiting sacrifice
by his own choice, keeping the day for the Sabbath or the discharge of a vow

schools.summary: David asks a weapon on the pretext of urgent haste; Ellicott sees him pointing to Goliath's own sword, once his votive offering, now needed back.
Goliath's sword lay behind the ephod, a prized dedicatory offering (17:2; 17:51), perhaps wrapped in the Philistine's own war-cloak.
Wesley and Clarke: 'none like it' — a trophy and a pledge of God's favour, a support to David's faith.

themes: what is devoted to God restored

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: David flees to Achish (Abimelech, the standing Philistine title, Psalm 34), hoping to pass unrecognized after the years since Goliath.
Henry: the anointed king an exile; providence seems to run counter to promise; God's people often find better usage among Philistines than Israelites.
Gill: fear of Saul, and of Doeg's report, drove David out of the land the same day.
Ellicott: David was known by the maidens' song, not the sword; his foes felt 'pitiful admiration' rather than rage.
Wesley: 'king of the land' means the king elect — the rumour of David's destined crown had spread even to Philistia.

debates.question: What is meant by 'David the king of the land'?

sides.position: a vague honorific for the renowned warrior; none there knew of his anointing (1 Samuel 16:13)
the king elect — the fame of his destined kingdom had reached even the Philistines

schools.summary: Ellicott: the reversal by which a life once held cheap grew precious; the Philistines' oddly generous silence about Goliath.
Gill: David ponders the words in his heart and weighs their consequence.
Wesley: fear of their revenge or policy, sharpened by his wearing Goliath's sword; better to trust the Lord than princes (Psalm 118:9).

themes: trust in the Lord not princes

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott describe the feigned madness; David knew its signs from Saul, and the scene stands behind the title of Psalm 34.
Clarke refuses to vindicate the deceit — 'if mocking be catching...' — and dares not be the apologist of lying.
Gill: David 'changed his taste,' acting as if bereft of reason or of speech.

debates.question: Was David's madness feigned or real?

sides.position: wholly feigned, as the sense of the narrative plainly indicates
possibly a real frenzy or epileptic fit (so the LXX's 'epileptic man')

themes: the low point of the flight

schools.summary: Gill: Achish thinks David mad — whether willing his servants to think so, or truly supposing Saul's pursuit had crazed him.
Ellicott: in the East the insane are held under God's protection, so the device made David perfectly safe; the Talmud's Ishbi-benob legend.
Gill: 'have I need of madmen?'; the tradition that Achish's own wife and daughter were mad.
Wesley: Achish likely knew it feigned but spoke to preserve David; Clarke rejects Chandler's defence of the deceit.
Keil and Delitzsch give the plain taunt: 'shall this fellow come into my house?'

debates.question: Did Achish see through David's feigned madness?

sides.position: yes — he knew it counterfeit but spoke as if real, to spare David
he may really have believed David deranged

themes: trust in the Lord not princes
